next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

By the time the actual parchment reached him, Owen had already been told twice that the Headmaster wanted him.

The first warning came from a narrow portrait on the second-floor landing, a witch in tarnished blue silk whose painted pearl earring had been scratched off years ago so that one bare ear looked oddly accusatory. She had been darning a stocking in her frame with short offended jabs. As Owen came up from the cellar after dawn, satchel dragging at his shoulder and the whole night with Prue still caked under his eyes, she looked up and said, "Mr Phipps. Do stop behaving as if staircases can protect you. The Headmaster has sent for you."

He had kept walking because portraits repeated things for pleasure and because he still had the Grinlow Leaves in his satchel, wrapped under his spare shirt beside the ash-linen notebook. He had not trusted the manuscript to the dormitory after the night's work, not with the map of four failing anchors still half alive in his head and Prue's last, flat silence following him down the corridor more cleanly than anything she might have said.

The second warning came outside the Great Hall. Edith Wren, a Hufflepuff sixth-year with a voice made papery by a winter cough, caught his sleeve as he went in. Her badge had been sewn back on with blue thread because she lost pins constantly and refused to admit it.

"Filch was asking after you before breakfast," she said. "And one of the Slytherin prefects. Proper asking, not corridor gossip."

"Right," Owen said.

Edith let go of his sleeve and studied his face for a second too lon...
open_hooks_to_carry: Keep the chapter physically small and temporally concentrated: one long night of work.
Translate the manuscript into a legible ward map with four anchors and Hogwarts as the most immediately stressed node.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...uched by the broken cistern, snapped a bit of plaster off the floor, and pressed it to the running crack as if damage still deserved practical attention even in the middle of betrayal.

"Len, get the wall. Hester, help him. Jo can come back up for the bucket. Nobody says press, sabotage, corridor, any of it if they're spoken to." He stood and faced Owen again. "And you don't come near another job of mine if you can't tell which side of it you're on."

Martha's blood was drying already in a crooked handprint where she had caught herself.

Owen said, because it was weak and because it was true, "I didn't mean this."

Neville's mouth went tight with something flatter than anger. "No. That's the problem."

He turned away and started giving people tasks. Hester fetched rags from the classroom. Len scraped soot and dung off the armor's decorative foot with the disgust of somebody cleaning up after a dog he did not own. Jo slipped back through the portrait with a bucket and would not meet Owen's eye. The corridor filled with work, quick and low-voiced and competent.

No one asked Owen to do any of it.

He stood by the still-open press with dirty water drying on his socks and the notebook heavy under his jumper while the others mopped, lifted, wiped, hid, and repaired around him. The cold from the service dark kept touching his ankle. Somewhere down the stair Amycus shouted, and farther off than that, almost below hearing, Martha made no sound at all.

By the time the last of them went back down to the kitchens in twos and threes, nobody had asked Owen to carry so much as a bucket.
state_deltas_required: The ward problem is now large enough that Owen needs Prue's help, but his priorities are becoming ethically indefensible to her.
Owen and Prue identify the four failing anchors, and Prue understands that his future power may not be used for the people in front of him.
